verses.commentary: The whole assembly rose, brought him before Pilate. Sanhedrin has no power of execution; Roman authority required.

Charges: 'We found this man perverting our nation, forbidding to give tribute to Caesar, saying he is Christ, a king.' Note the political reframing — the charges Pilate will care about are added beside the religious ones.
'Are you the King of the Jews?' 'You have said so.' Indirect affirmation.
Pilate to chief priests and crowds: 'I find no guilt (aition, αἴτιον) in this man.' First innocence declaration.
They were urgent: 'He stirs up the people, teaching throughout all Judea, from Galilee even to this place.' Mentioning Galilee gives Pilate his out.
Pilate, hearing Galilee, asked whether the man was a Galilean. Jurisdictional question.
Learning he was of Herod's jurisdiction (Antipas, ruler of Galilee), sent him to Herod, who was also in Jerusalem at the time. Passover brought Herod to the capital.
Herod, seeing Jesus, very glad — had long wanted to see him, hearing of him, hoping to see a sign. Curiosity, not faith.
Questioned him at length — Jesus made no answer. Silence as judgment on the trifler.
Chief priests and scribes stood, vehemently accusing him. The opposition is constant.
Herod with his soldiers treated him with contempt, mocked him, arrayed him in splendid clothing (esthēta lampran, ἐσθῆτα λαμπράν — bright robe, mock-royal). Sent him back to Pilate.
Herod and Pilate became friends that day — they had been enemies. Lukan ironic detail — Jesus reconciles even his enemies through being rejected by them.
Pilate called the chief priests, rulers, people together.
'You brought me this man as one perverting the people — I have examined him, found no guilt in your charges.'
'Neither did Herod, for he sent him back to us. Nothing deserving death has been done by him.' Innocence declaration by two officials.
'I will therefore punish (paideuo, παιδεύω — chastise/discipline, possibly euphemism for scourging) him and release him.' Compromise position.
[Textual variant absent from earliest manuscripts. Later manuscripts add: 'Now he had to release one man at the feast.' Harmonization with Matthew 27:15 / Mark 15:6.]
All together shouted: 'Away with this man! Release to us Barabbas!' Mob unified by hostility.
Barabbas — thrown into prison for an insurrection started in the city, and for murder. The actual political subversive released; the alleged subversive condemned.
Pilate addressed them again, desiring to release Jesus. Pilate's reluctance becomes the narrative emphasis.
They kept shouting: 'Crucify, crucify him!' The demand escalates to its specific form.
Third time: 'Why? What evil has he done? I have found no guilt deserving death. I will punish him and release him.' Pilate's third innocence declaration.
Urged with loud cries, demanding he be crucified — their voices prevailed. Mob wins by volume.
Pilate decided that their demand be granted. Capitulation.
Released the man for whom they asked — thrown in prison for insurrection and murder — and delivered Jesus over to their will. The exchange complete.
As they led him away, they seized Simon of Cyrene (North African Jew), coming in from the country, laid the cross on him to carry behind Jesus. Behind = following — conscripted disciple.
A great multitude followed, including women who were mourning and lamenting for him. Lament not from the disciples but from anonymous female mourners.
Turned and said: 'Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me — weep for yourselves and for your children.' Lukan unique. The lament is misdirected; the city's coming judgment is the deeper grief.
'Days are coming when they will say, Blessed are the barren, the wombs that never bore, the breasts that never nursed.' Reversal — usually barrenness was lamented; now it will be a mercy.
Then they will begin to say to the mountains, 'Fall on us,' and to the hills, 'Cover us.' (Hosea 10:8 echo.) Eschatological terror.
'For if they do these things when the wood is green, what will happen when it is dry?' Proverb: if this happens to the innocent, what will happen to the guilty city?
Two others, criminals (kakourgoi, κακοῦργοι), were led with him to be put to death. Lukan vocabulary distinct from Matthew's 'robbers.'

When they came to the place called The Skull (kranion, κρανίον — Latin: Calvaria) — there they crucified him and the criminals, one on his right and one on his left. The verb 'crucified' arrives without elaboration.

'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do.' Lukan unique — first of three crucifixion sayings only in Luke. Intercession for the executioners. (This verse is textually disputed but accepted by most editions.) Soldiers cast lots for his garments — Psalm 22:18.
The people stood by watching. Rulers scoffed: 'He saved others — let him save himself, if he is the Christ of God, his Chosen One.' Title 'Chosen One' echoes the transfiguration voice (9:35).
Soldiers mocked, offering him sour wine.
'If you are the King of the Jews, save yourself.' The 'if' echoes the wilderness temptation.
Inscription above him: 'This is the King of the Jews.' Titulus declared the truth even as accusation.
One of the criminals railed: 'Are you not the Christ? Save yourself and us.' Even at death, mockery.
The other rebuked him: 'Do you not fear God, since you are under the same sentence of condemnation?' Repentance starts with God-fear.
'We indeed justly — receiving the due reward of our deeds. But this man has done nothing wrong.' Two-stage repentance: self-condemnation + Christ-vindication.
'Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom.' Faith in dying. He sees the king on the cross.

'Truly I say to you, today (semeron, σήμερον) you will be with me in paradise (paradeiso, παραδείσῳ — Eden, restored).' Lukan unique. Salvation in the last hour. The first member of the church redeemed at the cross is a criminal.
About the sixth hour (noon) — darkness over the whole land until the ninth hour (3 pm).
The sun failed (eklipontos tou heliou, ἐκλιπόντος τοῦ ἡλίου — a possible reading; or 'the sun's light was darkened'). The temple curtain torn in two (down the middle). Holy place opened.

Jesus, crying with a loud voice: 'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit' (Psalm 31:5). Said this, breathed his last (exepneusen, ἐξέπνευσεν). Voluntary surrender. The dying word frames death as filial entrustment.

Centurion, seeing what had taken place, glorified God: 'Certainly this man was righteous (dikaios, δίκαιος).' Lukan version of the centurion confession — 'righteous' rather than 'Son of God.' The Roman witness vindicates Jesus.
All the crowds gathered for the spectacle, having seen what took place, returned home beating their breasts (typtontes ta stethe, τύπτοντες τὰ στήθη). The early signs of corporate repentance.
All his acquaintances and the women who had followed him from Galilee stood at a distance, watching these things. Faithful witnesses observe to the end.
A man named Joseph — member of the council (bouleutes, βουλευτής), good and righteous.
He had not consented to their decision and action. From Arimathea — also was waiting for the kingdom of God. Sanhedrin dissenter; covert disciple revealed.
Went to Pilate, asked for the body of Jesus. Roman protocol required permission.
Took it down, wrapped in linen shroud, laid in a rock-hewn tomb where no one had ever been laid. Unused tomb — ritual purity.
It was Preparation Day; Sabbath was beginning. Quick burial before sundown.
The women who had come with him from Galilee followed and saw the tomb and how his body was laid. They witnessed the burial location — necessary for the resurrection account.
Returned, prepared spices and ointments. On the Sabbath they rested according to the commandment. Torah-observance even during grief.
